{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.4, "R1.2": 0.6, "R1.3": 0.5, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.8}}

Summary: Critique of the related-work positioning, focused on whether the three-bucket framing actually isolates the contribution and whether the novelty claims survive contact with adjacent literatures the section currently omits.

Key issues with the positioning as written

1. The three buckets are too coarse and miss the most threatening adjacent work. The section defends novelty against (a) generic causal-inference methods, (b) descriptive surveys, and (c) pre-LLM quality control. None of these are the natural competitor. The closest prior work — and the one a reviewer will raise first — is the cluster of studies that already estimate LLM use among crowd workers using indirect detection or quasi-experimental variation, e.g., Veselovsky, Ribeiro & West's "Artificial Artificial Artificial Intelligence" (2023), which estimated 33–46% LLM use on MTurk summarization tasks, and follow-ups that use task-design changes (copy-paste disabling, keystroke logging) as natural experiments. Until these are named and distinguished, the "first credibly identified causal estimate" claim is exposed.

2. The instrument's novelty is asserted, not situated. Platform-level staggered rollouts as an IV for individual technology adoption is a well-established template (e.g., staggered Google/Facebook/broadband rollouts in labor and IO; staggered Copilot rollouts in software-engineering productivity studies such as Peng et al. 2023 and the GitHub Copilot field experiments). The contribution is the application, not the instrument class. The current text implies the instrument itself is new ("the first to instrument LLM exposure with platform-level rollouts"), which overclaims. Reframe as: novel application of a known IV template to a setting where the treatment (worker-side LLM routing) was previously unobservable.

3. The "quality" outcome is under-positioned. There is now a sizeable literature on LLM-vs-human and LLM-assisted annotation quality (Gilardi et al. 2023; Ziems et al. 2024; work on annotator drift and "silent contamination" of benchmarks). These papers measure quality differences directly, often via comparison to expert labels. Your design measures a LATE on fidelity scores — this is a different estimand, and the section should say so explicitly. Right now a reader cannot tell whether you are competing with these papers or complementing them.

4. The "pre-LLM quality control" bucket is a strawman. Reviewers will not accept that the gold-standard / IAA / qualification-test literature is the relevant comparison, because none of those papers ever claimed to address LLM contamination. Either drop this bucket or reframe it as motivating the outcome construction (how fidelity is operationalized) rather than as a contrast for the contribution.

5. The contribution claim is double-barreled and the second barrel is weaker. "First credibly identified causal estimate" and "first to instrument with platform rollouts" are presented as parallel, but they are not equally defensible (see #2). Consolidate into a single, narrower claim — e.g., "first LATE estimate of worker-side LLM adoption on annotation fidelity, using staggered assistant rollouts as an instrument" — and let the methodological framing follow.

6. Internal logic gap on the LATE interpretation. The section promises a LATE but never says for whom — compliers here are workers whose LLM use is shifted by browser-integration availability. This matters for positioning because the descriptive-prevalence literature estimates an ATE-like quantity over the full worker pool; the comparison is apples-to-oranges and should be flagged here, not deferred to Section 4.

7. Missing threads worth one sentence each: (a) the "model collapse" / training-data-contamination literature (Shumailov et al. 2024; Dohmatob et al.) which is the downstream stakes story; (b) audit studies of platform worker behavior under monitoring (relevant to instrument exogeneity); (c) the human-AI collaboration / augmentation literature on writing tasks (Noy & Zhang 2023; Dell'Acqua et al. 2023), which establishes that LLM assistance changes output distribution — your contribution is to ask whether that change is degradation in the annotation context specifically.

Concrete revision suggestions

- Replace bucket (a) with a tighter "IV with staggered rollouts" paragraph that cites the Copilot/broadband precedents and explicitly disclaims methodological novelty.
- Add a fourth bucket between (b) and (c): "Quasi-experimental and detection-based estimates of LLM use in crowdsourcing," anchored on Veselovsky et al. and successors. This is where the real novelty argument has to be won.
- Replace bucket (c) with "Annotation quality measurement," cite Gilardi/Ziems-style work, and use it to motivate the fidelity-score outcome rather than to contrast against.
- Soften "first to instrument LLM exposure with platform-level rollouts" to "first, to our knowledge, to apply a staggered-rollout IV to worker-side LLM adoption in annotation pipelines."
- Add one sentence naming the compliers and acknowledging the LATE-vs-prevalence distinction.

Uncertainty disclosure: Citation specifics (Veselovsky et al. 2023; Peng et al. 2023; Gilardi et al. 2023; Ziems et al. 2024; Shumailov et al. 2024; Noy & Zhang 2023; Dell'Acqua et al. 2023) are recalled from memory and should be verified for exact authorship, year, and venue before inclusion. The claim that no published paper has used staggered rollouts as an IV for crowd-worker LLM use specifically is plausible but not independently verified here.
